comments.text: Here in the Midwest, data centers threaten to suck up fresh water from our Great Lakes. Just one of may examples includes a Microsoft plant now under construction near Racine, WI that is expected to use upwards of 8 MILLION gallons a day. A day! That is not okay.

These data centers are coming to the Great Lakes basin because of our water. Humans should be concerned about the impact on the Great Lakes and explain the huge difference between gallons/day used and gallons/day discharged back into the lake. How will this affect me? 🌊🌊

"Large data centers, many devoted to researching artificial intelligence, are expected to use more than 150 billion gallons of water across the U.S. over the next five years, according to the advocacy organization Alliance for the Great Lakes. That’s enough water to supply 4.6 million homes."

Some of the most important water sources in the USA now have Data mills.
The Great Lakes, Texas Aquifers, and the Umatilla Basin here in Oregon. Amazon recently lost a case but only have to pay a paltry $20.5 million settlement for nitrate runoff destroying groundwater.
www.opb.org/article/2026...

AI is coming for the Great Lakes. The "world's most-powerful AI data center" in Wisconsin will gobble up 8.4 billion gallons. Even the average data center uses 300,000 gallons a day. Oh, and they're getting fat tax exemptions.
www.theguardian.com/technology/2...

Amazon's new $11B 1,200-acre data center in Indiana, near Lake Michigan. Operational as of November, it will use 2.2 gigawatts of power and 300 million gallons of water per year to run and train AI models from Anthropic.

Microsoft is also building an AI data center not terribly far from my bit of Michigan. It will draw two million gallons of water from Lake Michigan *per day*. To generate bullshit from stolen property.

'Data Centers Push Great Lakes Region to the Brink… The biggest data centers consume more than 365 million gallons of water annually, and data center water usage in the US tripled between 2014 and 2023, placing enormous strain on regional water resources.' oilprice.com/Energy/Energ...

They have. China has several. Microsoft had a test deployment up near Orkney (look up Project Natick). Related idea, Equinix has one built next to Lake Ontario and uses the deep water for cooling the data centre; Switzerland is looking at doing the same on Lake Geneva, iirc.

Draining the Great Lakes: Microsoft is building the “world’s most-powerful AI data center” that is set to open early next year and expected to use up to 8.4bn gallons of municipal water from the city of Racine every year. Racine gets its water from Lake Michigan. www.theguardian.com/technology/2...
Over 1 millions gallons of Great Lakes water DAILY. AI is a waste of resources! planetdetroit.org/2026/01/van-...

These centers are built to gulp millions of gallons of it. We can't let our #GreatLakes gift be drained for a server farm. And when a single facility uses the power of a small town, it doesn't just strain the grid—it threatens to blow a hole in your monthly budget.

Large data centers, many devoted to researching artificial intelligence, are expected to use more than 150 billion gallons of water across the U.S. over the next five years, according to the advocacy organization Alliance for the Great Lakes. That’s enough water to supply 4.6 million homes.
Meanwhile, in Ontario’s Great Lakes region — which provides water to 70 per cent of the province’s 14 million people — there are at least 108 data centres in the Greater Toronto Area alone.

Suit filed to learn Lake Michigan water use at Microsoft data center Tech companies have their eyes on the Great Lakes region due to freshwater resources, and their data centers can use as much water as a small city. www.jsonline.com/story/news/l...

I’m not a fan of data centers, but this is some wild fear mongering that makes it hard to take .com seriously Lake Erie has around 128 trillion gallons of water The Great Lakes combined have 6 QUADRILLION gallons

Yes, according to a report by the Alliance for the Great Lakes, a hyperscale data center can consume more than 365 million gallons annually, roughly the same as the water use of 12,000 Americans over the same time period:
Microsoft has access to the Great Lakes fresh water. It won’t be the last to try to suck the lakes dry.

Microsoft recently announced their siting a couple new datacenters in Racine County WI, one county South of me. I believe they're juuuuust inside the Lake Michigan watershed, same as the mostly abandoned Foxconn projects. Access to lake water must have been the important factor with both projects,

“tucked between the Boundary Waters Canoe Area & Lake Superior… the project would draw on 50,000 gallons of municipal water per day from Lake Superior—the equivalent demand of 160 homes. A single large data center can chug 5 million gallons of water per day—the equivalent of 50,000 homes.”

Fresh water to drink or AI? The giant data centers like Microsoft's in WI are locating themselves by bodies of fresh water for power cooling. The Great Lakes Water Quality Act between US & Canada requires both countries make sure the use of the Great Lakes water be free from harmful impact. Not!

Do you have an un-paywalled source? What does it mean to have high water stress? Why are locations along the Columbia river or on the Great Lakes listed as high water stress? How much of the water use on those areas is datacenters?

I have some issues with ai, but this isn’t it. The Great Lakes are the one place it actually might make sense to put data centers. There’s 6 quadrillion gallons of water available. I would like to see forecasts and studies on water temperature and resulting ecological impacts though.
